Confirmed. The detail-cell-renderer (one instance per expanded RFQ row) fires `getFOBCountry`, `loadDetailColumnHeaders`, and `getFactoryCountryMap` on every expansion — an N+1 pattern across rows for static reference data. `destroy$` in rfq.component is declared but never piped into any subscription.

I have comprehensive coverage. Let me consolidate the findings.

## Performance & Page-Load Findings — designflow-frontend

Ranked by impact. All paths relative to `C:\repos\dflow`.

### HIGH

**1. RFQ init is a serialized HTTP waterfall, not parallel** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts:658` (`ngOnInit`) → `loadRFQStatuses():745`. `ngOnInit` chains `verifyRole` → `getRFQItemStatus`, and only *inside* that subscribe (lines 750-756) does it fire `getRFQGroup`, `loadCustomers`, `loadDeliveryLocations`, `loadRFQSteps`, then `afterMetadataReady()` → `getAllRFQItem()`. These reference lists are independent but run sequentially behind one another, multiplying time-to-grid on the app's heaviest page. **Fix:** `forkJoin` the independent reference calls (status, group, customers, delivery, steps, CBM, license) and only chain `getAllRFQItem` after; this is the largest page-load win.

**2. RFQ subscriptions never unsubscribed (leak)** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` — `takeUntil` count is 0 despite ~30 `.subscribe()` calls (lines 746, 762, 815, 825, 843, 850, 857, 864, 880, 943, 4086, 4103, …). `destroy$` is declared and completed in `ngOnDestroy:401` but never piped into any stream. Every navigation to/from RFQ leaks all these subscriptions. **Fix:** add `.pipe(takeUntil(this.destroy$))` to every init/data subscription.

**3. itemDetail init is fully serialized + meaningless `await` on a Subscription** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemDetail/itemDetail.component.ts:210-472`. Chains `getDivisionCode` → `getSeasonCode` → `loadCustomerOptions` → `getMerchGroup` (line 230 does `await ...subscribe(...)`, which is a no-op await) and nests `getCompanyCode`, `getAllVendors`, `getItemDepth`, `getItemInfo` inside callbacks. Independent calls run one-after-another. Also `getItemInfo` is effectively fetched twice (line 195 stores `itemData`, line 454 reconsumes, plus child re-fetches). **Fix:** `firstValueFrom` + `forkJoin` the independent calls; flatten the chain; dedupe the item fetch.

**4. Detail-cell-renderer refetches static reference data per row expansion (N+1)** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts:131-142`. Each expanded RFQ row instantiates a renderer that calls `getFOBCountry`, `loadDetailColumnHeaders`, and `getFactoryCountryMap` — global static data fetched once per expanded row. **Fix:** cache these via `shareReplay(1)` in the service so expansions reuse a single response.

**5. Reference data (vendors/customers/RFQ groups/company codes) never cached** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts:63,75,105,165` and `customer.service.ts:23`. These raw `http.get` methods are called from ~70 sites (every grid page + every autocomplete) with no `shareReplay`; `CustomerService` even has a `customers$` store (line 13) that callers bypass. Full vendor/customer lists re-download on each navigation. **Fix:** expose memoized `shareReplay({bufferSize:1, refCount:false})` observables; route callers through them / the existing `customers$` store.

**6. standardized.component init is nested serial subscribes + client-side full-array rebuilds** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/standardized/standardized.component.ts:374-419`. `getSTDItems → getSTDHeader → getSelectedGridView` nested serially (3 round trips, none unsubscribed) before render; first two are independent. Uses client-side row model and `onBtnHighlight` (570-588) mutates every row then replaces the whole `rowData` array. **Fix:** `forkJoin` the independent calls + `takeUntil`; use `applyTransaction({update})` instead of full array replacement.

**7. Expensive getters/functions bound in templates (re-run every CD cycle)** — itemDetail.component.html:100,956 bind getters `timelineItems`/`teamMembers` (ts:924,937) that build new arrays each call; itemLibrary.component.html:233 binds getter `hoverTileGradient` (ts:1455) that hashes a string in a loop; art-piece-detail.component.html:306 calls `getPrimaryImage(item)` (`.find`) twice per related item. With default change detection these run on every event. **Fix:** precompute into fields when source data changes.

### MEDIUM

**8. No OnPush anywhere on heavy grid pages** — only 8 of 278 components use `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush`. rfq, prod_tracking, itemLibrary, licensing_tracking, standardized, itemDetail, art-piece(-detail), manage_users all use default CD with method/getter bindings (`getContextMenuItems`, `getRowStyle`, the getters in #7). Every mouse/keyboard/timer event re-evaluates the whole tree. **Fix:** adopt OnPush + drive updates through the already-injected `cdr`.

**9. AG Grid Enterprise registers ALL modules at bootstrap** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/app.module.ts:41-42` `ModuleRegistry.registerModules([AllEnterpriseModule])` at module-eval pulls the entire enterprise bundle into initial load even on `/login`, `/notifications`, factory form. **Fix:** register only the modules used, or move registration into lazy grid feature modules.

**10. Heavy third-party modules eagerly imported in root AppModule** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/app.module.ts:82-98` eagerly imports `QuillModule.forRoot`, `CalendarModule.forRoot` (+date-fns), `CarouselModule`, `FontAwesomeModule`, `MatMomentDateModule`. Only needed on specific pages but paid for on every initial load. **Fix:** move into the lazy modules that use them.

**11. license-tracking: cubic per-row enrichment + leaks** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemDetail/license-tracking/license-tracking.component.ts:149-208,325-335`. `enrichRowWithGroupInfo` does `groupsList.find` then per-member `usersList.find` → O(rows × members × users). Subscriptions (101,109,139) have no teardown and no `ngOnDestroy`. **Fix:** build `Map` lookups once; add `destroy$`/`takeUntil`.

**12. itemLibrary: full dataset re-fetched on every cell edit** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemLibrary/itemLibrary.component.ts:1144-1163`. `onCellValueChanged` calls `getAllItem(...)` (to build undo state) plus `updateRfqItem` on every single edit — a large duplicate fetch per keystroke-commit. **Fix:** derive undo state from the in-memory row.

**13. art-piece-detail: serial init + over-broad refetch on mutations** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/art-piece/art-piece-detail/art-piece-detail.component.ts:112-127`. `getSeasonCode → getCompanyCode → getArtPieceInfo → getRelatedItems` awaited serially (first three independent); every image mutation (setPrimary/delete/upload/save) calls `getArtPieceInfo` which re-fetches the whole merch-group dataset (line 145). Uses deprecated `.toPromise()` (117,118,131,145,265). **Fix:** parallelize init; refresh only attachments on image ops.

**14. Subscriptions/HTTP fired from constructors, never torn down** — `prod_tracking.component.ts:174`, `standardized.component.ts:241`, `manage_users.component.ts:177` all call `getCustomGridLayoutList(...).subscribe(...)` in the constructor with no cleanup. **Fix:** move to ngOnInit + `takeUntil`.

**15. manage_users destroys/recreates the entire grid on filter change** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/manage_users/manage_users.component.ts:899-905`. `onUserTypeChange` toggles `isGridVisible` off then on via `setTimeout(...,0)`, tearing down and rebuilding the whole grid instead of reloading rows. Also `destroy$` (line 104) is never completed, so `gridUISetupService.bootstrap` teardown (737) never fires. **Fix:** reload data only; complete `destroy$` in `ngOnDestroy`.

**16. itemLibrary merch-group valueGetter does linear scans per cell** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemLibrary/itemLibrary.component.ts:706-741`. The `valueGetter` from `mapHeaderValues` runs `allMerchGroups.find(...)` per cell on every scroll/refresh → O(cells × groups). **Fix:** precompute a `Map` keyed by id/code.

### LOW

**17. Export loops fetch images one-at-a-time (N+1)** — `manage_users.component.ts:612-617` and `art-piece.component.ts:1011-1023` `await getBase64FromUrl` per row in a loop. Export-time only. **Fix:** `Promise.all`.

**18. UserAuthInterceptor reads localStorage synchronously per request** — `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/interceptor/user-auth.interceptor.ts:15`. `localStorage.getItem('token')` on every outbound request (mirrored in notification.service.ts:47, main.service.ts:16/22). Adds up under parallel grid requests. **Fix:** cache token in memory, update on login/logout.

**19. Toolbar popover `afterClosed$` subscriptions leak per interaction** — `designflow-frontend/src/@vex/layout/toolbar/toolbar-notifications/toolbar-notifications.component.ts:49` and `toolbar-user/toolbar-user.component.ts:55`. No `take(1)`/`untilDestroyed` on the long-lived singleton toolbar. **Fix:** `take(1)`.

**20. `@for` track-by-object on dropdown/option lists** — across prod_tracking, itemLibrary, standardized, art-piece, itemDetail templates (e.g. `track value`, `track data`, `track option`). Tracking object references defeats reuse when arrays are re-sorted/recreated. Lists are small, so low impact. **Fix:** `track x.id`.

### Notes (verified non-issues)
- Routing is fully lazy-loaded with `QuicklinkStrategy` preloading — good. Note `onSameUrlNavigation: 'reload'` (app-routing.module.ts:364) forces full re-resolution on same-URL nav.
- `NotificationService` polling (30s, `exhaustMap`+`startWith`, proper `ngOnDestroy`) is well-built — only nit is no pause-on-tab-hidden and `getMentionableUsers` isn't cached.
- prod_tracking/itemLibrary/licensing use server-side row model with pagination — virtualization is fine; only `standardized` is client-side. `custom-layout`/`layout` correctly use `@UntilDestroy`.

All six review agents are done. Here's the consolidated deep-dive across all six repos (the directory has 6 service repos under `dflow`, not 7 — `.claude` is the config folder).

## The headline issues

**🔴 Auth bypass in the backend (fix today)** — [helpers/auth.js:4](designflow-backend/helpers/auth.js:4) sets `verifyOptions = { algorithm: ["RS256"] }`. The `jsonwebtoken` library reads the **plural** `algorithms`; the singular is silently ignored, so the algorithm allow-list is never enforced. Since `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` is public, an attacker can forge an `HS256` token signed with the public key as the HMAC secret and pass `jwt.verify`. This compromises the entire auth layer including the admin-gated impersonation route. Fix: `algorithms: ["RS256"]`.

**🔴 Live API key committed to source** — [models/lib.model.js:3541](designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js:3541) has a hardcoded live Sendinblue/Brevo key (`xkeysib-419f7a93...`). It's in git history. Rotate it now and move to Secret Manager. (Frontend has the same class of issue with the Cold Lion key handling.)

**🔴 Two guaranteed runtime crashes in data-syncing** — [models/lib.model.js:835](designflow-data-syncing/models/lib.model.js:835) and `:867` reference an undefined variable `i` inside `getExternalVendor`/`getExternalCustomer` — `ReferenceError` on every non-empty sync (dead-on-arrival copy-paste). And [models/lib.model.js:446](designflow-data-syncing/models/lib.model.js:446)'s `retryUpload` uses `while (j < 5 || ...)` so it never exits early on success and compares `content-length` to the number `200` (an HTTP status, not a length).

## Pages loading slower than they should (your specific ask)

The frontend's heaviest pages are slow for compounding reasons:

1. **Serialized HTTP waterfalls on init.** The RFQ page ([rfq.component.ts:658](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts:658)), itemDetail ([itemDetail.component.ts:210](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemDetail/itemDetail.component.ts:210)), standardized, and art-piece-detail all chain independent reference-data calls one-inside-another's `.subscribe()` callback instead of firing them together. RFQ in particular runs ~5 independent lookups in sequence before the grid even starts loading. **Fix: `forkJoin` the independent calls.** This is the single biggest page-load win.

2. **The unbounded backend query behind that grid.** [models/lib.model.js:450](designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js:450) (`getAll`) does a `findAll` with **no limit** across a ~10-table association tree (nested Factory in RFQVendors) on every RFQ grid load. The frontend waterfall is waiting on this. Needs server-side pagination.

3. **Reference data is never cached.** Vendors, customers, RFQ groups, company codes ([main.service.ts](designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts), [customer.service.ts:23](designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/customer.service.ts:23)) are re-fetched from ~70 call sites on every navigation — there's even an unused `customers$` store callers bypass. Add `shareReplay(1)`.

4. **Per-row refetches (N+1 on the client).** Expanding an RFQ row re-fetches global static data per row ([detail-cell-renderer.component.ts:131](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts:131)); editing a cell in itemLibrary re-fetches the **entire dataset** to build undo state ([itemLibrary.component.ts:1144](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemLibrary/itemLibrary.component.ts:1144)).

5. **No change-detection discipline.** Only 8 of 278 components use OnPush, and templates bind getters/functions that rebuild arrays or hash strings every CD cycle ([itemDetail.component.html:100](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemDetail/itemDetail.component.html:100), [itemLibrary.component.ts:706](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/itemLibrary/itemLibrary.component.ts:706) does `allMerchGroups.find()` per cell on every scroll). Every mouse/keyboard event re-runs all of this.

6. **Eager bundle bloat.** [app.module.ts:41](designflow-frontend/src/app/app.module.ts:41) registers `AllEnterpriseModule` (entire AG Grid Enterprise) plus Quill, Calendar+date-fns, Carousel, FontAwesome, MatMoment at bootstrap — paid for even on `/login`. Move into lazy feature modules. (Routing itself is properly lazy-loaded — that part's good.)

## Two bugs that hang the UI / requests

- **Frontend auth guard hangs forever on a failed token check.** [auth.service.ts:54](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/auth/helpers/auth.service.ts:54) — `verifyToken`'s subscribe has no error callback, so any 401/500/network error means `resolve()` is never called and the user sits on a blank screen instead of going to login. Add an `error: () => resolve(false)`.
- **Tracking request hangs on missing data.** [lib.model.js:307](designflow-tracking/models/lib.model.js:307) `getViewlayout` has no try/catch and dereferences `GridLayouts[0].dataValues`; an empty layout throws inside `.then`, `result` is never called, request hangs until timeout.

## Pervasive cross-service anti-patterns

These repeat across the Node services and are worth one coordinated fix:

- **Errors returned as HTTP 200.** The `result(null, err)` / `return { message }` pattern reports DB failures as success in item-master ([item_library.service.js:334](designflow-item-master/services/item_library.service.js:334)), tracking ([lib.model.js:278](designflow-tracking/models/lib.model.js:278)), and backend service layers. Clients can't tell success from failure.
- **`sequelize.authenticate()` never awaited** in db.js across backend, data-syncing, item-master, tracking — the try/catch is dead, failures become unhandled rejections, and "Connection established" logs even on failure. data-syncing additionally fires `sequelize.sync()` unawaited in production (can auto-alter your schema).
- **Swallowed errors.** data-syncing logs every axios error as `console.log(console.log(error))` (prints `undefined`, swallows the rejection) — [lib.model.js:48](designflow-data-syncing/models/lib.model.js:48) and many more.
- **Leftover `logging: console.log`** on hot query paths in backend and item-master dumps full SQL + bound values on every call.
- **N+1 / row-by-row writes** instead of `bulkCreate(..., {updateOnDuplicate})` throughout data-syncing's sync loops ([getProdOrderDetail](designflow-data-syncing/models/lib.model.js:897) re-fetches every order every run with no delta filter) and tracking's `Box.addSamples` ([sampleGroup.model.js:209](designflow-tracking/models/sampleGroup.model.js:209)).

## BFF
Cleanest repo (the SQL migrations are genuinely well-written — parameterized, RLS-preserving). Two real issues: **proxy timeouts disabled** (`proxyTimeout: 0, timeout: 0` at [api.js:9](designflow-bff/routes/api.js:9)) means a hung backend ties up the BFF forever; and an **OIDC token is fetched from Google on every single proxied request** ([backendProxy.js:30](designflow-bff/src/backendProxy.js:30)) — tokens last ~1h, so cache them. Also CORS defaults to reflecting any origin with credentials when `CORS_ORIGINS` is unset.

---

**If I were to prioritize:** (1) the JWT `algorithms` fix and key rotation — security; (2) the two data-syncing crash bugs — currently broken; (3) RFQ `forkJoin` + backend pagination — your biggest user-facing speed win; (4) frontend reference-data caching + the hanging auth guard.

Want me to fix any of these? I'd suggest starting with the auth bypass and the two crash bugs since they're small, contained, and high-impact — I can do those in one pass. The performance refactors (forkJoin, pagination, caching) are larger and I'd tackle them per-page.

# CLAUDE.md — designflow-data-syncing

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first — it is the canonical guide. This file holds only
Claude Code-specific notes. For cross-repo infrastructure/runtime standards, see
[u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md)
and update it when Cloud Run, Cloud Build, domains, BFF routing, secrets/env,
storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating rules change.

## Memory and branch

- User memory: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`. Work only on `sandbox-albert` (and `develop` for syncing).
  Before new code, confirm local `sandbox-albert`, fetch GitHub, merge `origin/develop` into
  local `sandbox-albert`, push that to GitHub, then pull `sandbox-albert` back locally before
  editing. Never commit to or merge into `main`. Commit locally, push to
  `origin/sandbox-albert`, then ship via PR `sandbox-albert` → `develop`, updating or creating
  it after pushes.

## Ignore guidance

Use `.claudeignore` (mirrors `.cursorignore`). It excludes `node_modules/`, caches, logs,
`uploads/`, Playwright artifacts, and `.env*` from AI context.

## Allowed operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, `npm install`, `npm test`, `node`, `git add/commit`,
  `git push origin sandbox-albert`.
- Not allowed: editing `node_modules/`; `git push --force`; committing to `main`.

## Tests

- `npm test` (Jest, `tests/**/*.test.js`). Most `start:*` scripts run `npm test` first;
  `start:sandbox-albert` does **not**, so run it yourself before committing.
- Add/extend a unit test for any function you add or change.

## Deployment / SSH

Deployment is Cloud Build → Artifact Registry → Cloud Run (see `AGENTS.md` §7). The service is
private (`--no-allow-unauthenticated`). SSH is **not** part of the workflow.

## Commit style

```
type(scope): short description
```
Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `chore`. Scope e.g. `sync`, `coldlion`, `migration`.

## Claude-specific behaviors

- Quote mixed-case identifiers in raw `ALTER TABLE` DDL (`models/db.js`) — see AGENTS.md §11.
- Never bulk-load the DB through a Windows console (CP437 mojibake risk) — see AGENTS.md §11/§12.
- Keep `models/db/` in sync with `designflow-backend` when changing schema (no shared package).

# CLAUDE.md — Claude Code notes for designflow-frontend

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first. It is the canonical guide for repo structure, custom-code boundaries, current architecture, incidents, deployment, and pending work. This file is only for Claude Code-specific operating notes. For cross-repo infrastructure/runtime standards, see [u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md) and update it when Cloud Run, Cloud Build, domains, BFF routing, secrets/env, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating rules change.

## Memory and Branch

- User memory file: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`
- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert`
- Before new code, confirm local `sandbox-albert`, fetch GitHub, merge `origin/develop` into local `sandbox-albert`, push that updated `sandbox-albert` to GitHub, then pull GitHub `sandbox-albert` locally before editing.
- Work only on `sandbox-albert`; use `develop` only for sync/PR base. Never commit to, merge into, or open PRs against `main`.
- Commit locally on `sandbox-albert`, push to `origin/sandbox-albert`, then keep the GitHub PR `sandbox-albert` -> `develop` updated, or create it if missing.

## Ignore Guidance

Use `.claudeignore`. It matches `.cursorignore` and excludes generated, vendored, cache, asset, and legacy/demo template paths such as `dist/`, `node_modules/`, `.angular/`, `.cache/`, `.yarn/`, `coverage/`, `src/assets/`, `src/static-data/`, and Vex demo pages.

## Allowed Operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `yarn`/`npm` scripts, run `ng`, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- Not allowed by default: force-push, rewrite history, edit `node_modules/` or `dist/`
- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage.

## Tests

```powershell
yarn test
yarn test --testPathPatterns="rfq.grid.config"
yarn test:watch
```

Run the relevant tests before committing. Add or update unit tests for every new or changed function/behavior. The Docker build also runs `yarn test`.

## Claude-Specific Rules

- Use the AG Grid MCP docs before touching AG Grid code: set version `35.1.0`, framework `angular`; the repo uses AG Grid `35.2.1`.
- Do not use `.toPromise()` in new code; use `firstValueFrom()`.
- Do not add new `window['anything']` globals; use Angular DI.
- Do not add `aggFunc` to RFQ grid columns without reading `AGENTS.md`.
- For `mat-select`, keep options in a synchronous array, do not mix `[formControl]` with `[(value)]`, and never add `(click)="select.open()"`.

## Commit Style

```text
type(scope): short description
```

Use `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, or `chore`. Keep messages specific.

## Tests

  (`pages/sample_tracking/helpers/sample.status.ts`) in sync.

# CLAUDE.md — Claude Code Session Guide

Read `AGENTS.md` first. This file covers Claude-specific context only. For cross-repo infrastructure/runtime standards, see [u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md) and update it when Cloud Run, Cloud Build, domains, BFF routing, secrets/env, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating rules change.

---

## Active branch

`sandbox-albert`. Never merge or switch branches. All commits go to `sandbox-albert` only.

---

## Operations permissions

- **Allowed:** Read, edit, write files in `src/`, `routes/`, `config/`, `env/`, root config files
- **Allowed:** `git add`, `git commit`, `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- **Allowed:** `npm install`, `node`, `npm test`
- **Not allowed:** `git merge`, `git checkout <branch>`, `git push --force`
- **Not allowed:** Editing files in `node_modules/`

---

## Running tests

```bash
npm test             # Jest unit tests
npx jest --watch     # Watch mode
```

Tests must pass before every commit. CI runs `npm test` inside the Docker build — a failing test fails the build and blocks deployment.

---

## Commit style

```
type(scope): short description

Body if needed.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, `chore`. Scope examples: `proxy`, `auth`, `env`, `deploy`.

---

## GCP operations

When running `gcloud` commands:
```bash
export PATH="$PATH:/c/Users/ahazan2/AppData/Local/Google/Cloud SDK/google-cloud-sdk/bin"
```

GCP project: `lithe-breaker-323913`, region: `us-central1`.

Use the Bash tool (not PowerShell) for `gcloud` commands — the SDK is in a Unix-style path.

# CLAUDE.md — Claude Code notes for designflow-backend

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first. It is the canonical guide for repo structure, custom-code boundaries, data model, migrations, deployment, incidents, and pending work. This file is only for Claude Code-specific operating notes. For cross-repo infrastructure/runtime standards, see [u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md) and update it when Cloud Run, Cloud Build, domains, BFF routing, secrets/env, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating rules change.

## Memory and Branch

- User memory file: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`
- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert`
- Merge or switch branches only when the user explicitly asks; normal delivery is PR to `develop`

## Ignore Guidance

Use `.claudeignore`. It matches `.cursorignore` and excludes generated, vendored, cache, Playwright, runtime upload, and large data paths such as `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, `dist/`, `.cache/`, `images/`, `thumbs/`, and `data/hts-rates.json`.

## Allowed Operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `npm` scripts, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- Not allowed by default: force-push, rewrite history, edit `node_modules/`
- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage.

## Tests

```powershell
npm test
npm test -- --testPathPattern="<name>"
```

Run the relevant tests before committing. `start:sandbox-albert` does not run tests before starting `nodemon`.

## Claude-Specific Rules

- For DB columns: model field + `models/db.js` inline migration + `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` assertion.
- Quote mixed-case Postgres column identifiers in inline migrations.
- Add code to `routes/lib.router.js` and `models/lib.model.js` surgically; they are large legacy files.
- Keep secrets out of `--set-env-vars`; use Secret Manager via `--set-secrets`.

## Commit Style

```text
type(scope): short description
```

Use `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, or `chore`. Keep messages specific.

If the user wants to reschedule, edit the prompt, or pause/resume a task that already exists, call the `update_scheduled_task` tool with its `taskId` — do **not** call `create_scheduled_task`. ## Creating a new task

You are distilling the current session into a reusable shortcut. Follow these steps:

### 1. Analyze the session

Review the session history to identify the core task the user performed or requested. Distill it into a single, repeatable objective.

### 2. Draft a prompt

The prompt will be used for future autonomous runs — it must be entirely self-contained. Future runs will NOT have access to this session, so never reference "the current conversation," "the above," or any ephemeral context.

Include in the description:
- A clear objective statement (what to accomplish)
- Specific steps to execute
- Any relevant file paths, URLs, repositories, or tool names
- Expected output or success criteria
- Any constraints or preferences the user expressed

Write the description in second-person imperative ("Check the inbox…", "Run the test suite…"). Keep it concise but complete enough that another Claude session could execute it cold.

### 3. Choose a taskName

Pick a short, descriptive name in kebab-case (e.g. "daily-inbox-summary", "weekly-dep-audit", "format-pr-description").

### 4. Determine scheduling

The `create_scheduled_task` tool description explains the options (`cronExpression` for recurring, `fireAt` for one-time, omit both for ad-hoc) and their formats. If the user didn't give a clear schedule, propose one and ask them to confirm before proceeding.

input.description: Reminder: fix the JWT auth-bypass typo in designflow-backend once the developer signs off
input.prompt:
This is a reminder for the user (u2giants@gmail.com).

Remind them: yesterday we deferred fixing a JWT authentication-bypass vulnerability in the DesignFlow backend, pending the developer confirming it won't break his tokens. Check whether that sign-off has happened, and if so, offer to apply the fix.

The bug: in `C:\repos\dflow\designflow-backend\helpers\auth.js` (around line 4), the code is:
  `var verifyOptions = { algorithm: ["RS256"] };`
The `jsonwebtoken` library reads the PLURAL option name `algorithms`; the singular `algorithm` is silently ignored, so the allowed-algorithm allow-list is never enforced. Because `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` is public, an attacker can forge an HS256 token signed with the public key as the HMAC secret and pass `jwt.verify`. This affects the entire auth layer (`authRole`, `getUserInfo`) including the admin-gated impersonation route.

The fix is a one-line change: `var verifyOptions = { algorithms: ["RS256"] };`

Steps:
1. Greet the user and remind them of the above context.
2. Ask whether the developer has confirmed that enforcing RS256-only is safe for existing tokens.
3. If yes, apply the one-line fix in helpers/auth.js, run the backend tests, and report. Work on branch `sandbox-albert`. Do not commit unless asked.
4. If not yet confirmed, ask whether they want to be reminded again later.

content.text:
Everything's done except #8, which needs your decision. Here's the full rundown.

## ✅ Done — code changes

**#1 — Two crashes in data-syncing** ([lib.model.js](designflow-data-syncing/models/lib.model.js))
Fixed `getExternalVendor` and `getExternalCustomer`. The `data[i]` referenced an undefined `i` (guaranteed `ReferenceError`), and `updateData` applied one row's `where` clause to *every* row. Both now wrap in try/catch and call `updateData(rows, table, keyField)`, which updates each row by its own key with the `modTime` "only if newer" guard preserved. Syntax-checked OK.

**#5 — Two hang bugs**
- Frontend [auth.service.ts:54](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/auth/helpers/auth.service.ts:54): `verifyToken` now has an `error: () => resolve(false)` callback, so a failed `/verifyToken` redirects to login instead of hanging the guard on a blank screen.
- Tracking [lib.model.js:307](designflow-tracking/models/lib.model.js:307): `getViewlayout` wrapped in try/catch and guards empty `GridLayouts` before indexing `[0]`, so a missing Default layout returns cleanly instead of an unhandled rejection that never calls `result`.

**#7 — BFF changes** (all four, syntax-checked)
- [api.js](designflow-bff/routes/api.js): proxy timeouts now `30s`/`31s` (env-overridable) instead of `0`/`0`; removed the dead `err.response` passthrough that could never fire.
- [backendProxy.js](designflow-bff/src/backendProxy.js): OIDC `IdTokenClient` cached per audience in a `Map` (was created + token re-fetched from Google on every request; failed clients aren't cached).
- [apiKeyAuth.js](designflow-bff/src/apiKeyAuth.js): parsed/hashed API keys memoized once instead of re-parsing `DESIGNFLOW_API_KEYS` per request.
- [server.js](designflow-bff/src/server.js): CORS now fails closed — permissive reflection only for `localhost`; other envs require an explicit `CORS_ORIGINS` allowlist (warns if unset).

⚠️ I couldn't run the jest suites — none of these repos have `node_modules` installed. I syntax-checked every edited file with `node --check` (all pass). Worth a `yarn/npm test` before you ship.

**#2 — Sendinblue/Brevo key**
- Stored the key in 1Password (`vibe_coding` vault, item "Sendinblue (Brevo) API Key - designflow-backend", tagged for rotation).
- [lib.model.js:3542](designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js:3542) now reads `brevoConfig.API_KEY` (i.e. `process.env.BREVO_API_KEY`) — reusing the repo's existing `config/brevo.config.js` pattern that `helpers/brevo.js` already uses. The hardcoded literal is gone.
- **Two things you still need to do manually:** (1) The key is in git history, so **rotate it in Brevo** and update both the 1Password item and the Cloud Run secret. (2) Provision `BREVO_API_KEY` to the backend Cloud Run service via Secret Manager (`--set-secrets`), per your backend CLAUDE.md — it's in `.env.example` but confirm it's actually wired in prod.

**#3 — Reminder**
One-time scheduled task `remind-jwt-authbypass-fix` set for **tomorrow 9:00 AM**. It'll ask if the developer signed off and offer to apply the `algorithm` → `algorithms` one-liner. (Note: it only fires while the app is open; if closed, it runs on next launch.)

---

## 📋 #4 — The 6 page-load changes, point by point

**1. `forkJoin` the serialized init waterfalls (RFQ, itemDetail, standardized, art-piece)**
- *Effect:* Largest single win. RFQ fires ~5 independent reference calls sequentially today; in parallel, time-to-grid drops from roughly the sum of those latencies to the slowest one (often a 3–5× reduction on that phase).
- *Advantages:* Big perceived-speed gain; no API/backend changes needed; localized to each component's `ngOnInit`.
- *Disadvantages/risk:* Must carefully separate truly-independent calls from ones that depend on a prior result (e.g. `getAllRFQItem` must still wait for metadata). Error handling changes — `forkJoin` errors if *any* stream errors, so you need per-stream `catchError(() => of(default))` or one failure blanks the whole page. Medium effort, medium regression risk; test each page.

**2. Add server-side pagination to the RFQ `getAll` query**
- *Effect:* This is the backend half of RFQ slowness — an unbounded `findAll` across ~10 joined tables. Capping it is the difference between transferring the whole table and one page.
- *Advantages:* Cuts DB time, payload size, and client parse/render time all at once; scales as data grows.
- *Disadvantages/risk:* **Highest effort and risk** of the six — it's a contract change. The AG Grid client must switch to server-side/infinite row model (or send page params), and sorting/filtering/search must move server-side to stay correct. Touches both repos and the grid config. Do this as its own change with thorough testing; don't bundle it.

**3. Cache reference data with `shareReplay(1)` (vendors, customers, RFQ groups, company codes)**
- *Effect:* Eliminates dozens of duplicate downloads per navigation; autocompletes and grids become instant after first load.
- *Advantages:* Low effort, high impact, low risk; there's even an unused `customers$` store to route through.
- *Disadvantages/risk:* Staleness — cached lists won't reflect a newly-added vendor until refresh. Mitigate with `refCount` or a manual invalidate on create/edit. Decide TTL vs. session-lifetime per dataset.

**4. Cache per-row-expansion fetches (RFQ detail-cell-renderer)**
- *Effect:* Removes a client-side N+1 — expanding 20 rows currently makes 20× the same static-reference calls.
- *Advantages:* Low effort (same `shareReplay` technique in the service); noticeable when users expand many rows.
- *Disadvantages/risk:* Minimal — same staleness caveat as #3, but this is genuinely static data so risk is very low.

**5. Adopt OnPush + precompute template getters**
- *Effect:* Today only 8/278 components use OnPush, and templates call getters/`.find()` per cell on every change-detection cycle. OnPush + precomputed fields cut CD work dramatically on grid pages.
- *Advantages:* Smoother scroll/typing/interaction on heavy pages; scales with grid size.
- *Disadvantages/risk:* **Most invasive behaviorally.** OnPush changes when views update — anything relying on mutation-in-place instead of new references will silently stop refreshing, requiring `markForCheck()` or immutable updates. Roll out per-component with testing, not globally at once. High cumulative effort.

**6. Trim the eager bundle (lazy-load AG Grid Enterprise, Quill, Calendar, Carousel, FontAwesome)**
- *Effect:* Shrinks the initial JS downloaded/parsed before *anything* renders — helps first load and login, especially on slow connections.
- *Advantages:* Faster cold start for every user; routing is already lazy so the pattern's established.
- *Disadvantages/risk:* `AllEnterpriseModule` → selective module registration needs verifying every grid feature in use still works (a missing module breaks a grid at runtime, not build time). Moving `forRoot` imports into lazy feature modules is mechanical but must be exhaustive. Medium effort, medium risk; measure with a bundle analyzer before/after.

**Suggested order:** #3 + #4 first (cheap, safe, immediate), then #1 (big win, moderate care), then #6, then #2 and #5 as their own carefully-tested projects.

---

## 📋 #6 — N+1 / row-by-row writes → `bulkCreate`, point by point

**What it changes:** loops doing one `INSERT`/`UPDATE` per row (and often a per-row `SELECT` first) become a single `bulkCreate(rows, { updateOnDuplicate: [...] })` or batched `findAll({ where: { [Op.in]: keys } })`. Affects data-syncing sync loops, tracking's `Box.addSamples`, and several backend write paths.

**Advantages**
- *Throughput:* The dominant cost is network round-trips to the DB, not the SQL itself. 1,000 rows × (1 SELECT + 1 write) ≈ 2,000 round-trips becomes 1–2 statements. For the syncs (which re-process everything each run), this is often a 10–50× wall-clock improvement.
- *Connection-pool relief:* Sequential awaits hold a connection far longer; batching frees the pool (`max: 22`) and reduces the chance of acquire-timeouts.
- *Atomicity option:* A bulk write wrapped in a transaction either fully lands or fully rolls back — fixes the current "partial state on mid-loop failure" problem (e.g. `getProdOrderDetail` abandons remaining orders on error).

**Disadvantages / risks**
- *Behavioral nuance with `ignoreDuplicates`/`updateOnDuplicate`:* You must declare a real unique constraint for upsert to work, and decide exactly which columns get overwritten. Get the column list wrong and you either clobber data you meant to keep or silently skip updates. This is the #1 source of subtle bugs in this migration.
- *Conditional updates are harder to express:* The current row-by-row code can do "update only if incoming `modTime` is newer." `updateOnDuplicate` overwrites unconditionally unless you add a SQL `CASE`/literal — so naive batching can resurrect stale data. (This is exactly why my #1 fix kept the per-row conditional update rather than forcing a bulk upsert.)
- *Per-row hooks/side effects:* If any model has Sequelize hooks or the loop does per-row work (Teams notifications, audit writes), `bulkCreate` skips individual hooks unless `individualHooks: true` (which removes most of the speed benefit). Each loop needs auditing for side effects.
- *Memory:* Bulk operations load the whole batch into memory. For genuinely huge datasets you still need chunking (e.g. 1–5k rows per batch) — it's bulk *within bounded batches*, not one giant statement.
- *Parameter limits:* Postgres caps bound parameters (~65k); very wide rows × large batches can exceed it, forcing a batch-size cap.

**Effect / bottom line:** High payoff for the sync services specifically (they're the throughput-bound ones), but it's not a blind find-replace — each site needs a unique constraint, an explicit `updateOnDuplicate` column list, a decision on conditional-update semantics, and a transaction. Best done service-by-service with the existing data verified against the upsert keys first. Medium effort, medium risk, high reward where it matters.

---

## ❓ #8 — RFQ archive of oldest 1500 rows: I need your go-ahead

I investigated rather than executed, because this is a production-data change. Findings:

- **"Archive" here is a reversible flag, not a delete.** For internal roles, archiving sets `RFQItem.rfqItem_archive = 1` (Unarchive sets it back to null) via the `/archiveRFQItem` endpoint. So it's relatively safe — but it **hides those items from the active RFQ/sourcing views** for all internal users, which is a real operational impact.
- **"Last changed" exists:** the column is `rfqItem_date_modified` (set to `now()` on update). Caveat: it's **nullable** — rows never modified, or created before the column existed, may be NULL, so I'd need a rule for those (treat NULL as oldest? exclude them? fall back to `rfqItem_created_date`?).
- **Blocker: I have no DB access from this checkout.** Only `.env.example` is present — no real `DB_HOST`/credentials. So I literally cannot run the update from here.

Before I go further, I need a couple of decisions:

questions.question: How do you want the RFQ archive of the oldest 1500 rows executed?

options.label: Write a reviewable script
options.description: I write a standalone Node script (using the repo's models) or a parameterized SQL statement that archives the oldest 1500 by rfqItem_date_modified. You review it, then run it yourself against the DB with real credentials. Safest — you control execution.

options.label: I'll provide DB access
options.description: You give me a connection/credentials (e.g. via 1Password) and I run it directly here after you confirm the exact row set. Faster but I'm mutating production data.

questions.question: How should rows with a NULL rfqItem_date_modified (never modified / pre-column) be treated when picking the oldest 1500?

options.label: Fall back to created_date
options.description: Use rfqItem_created_date when rfqItem_date_modified is NULL, so 'last changed' approximates to creation time for unmodified rows. Most intuitive definition of 'oldest'.

options.label: Treat NULL as oldest
options.description: Rows with no modified date are considered the oldest and archived first. Simple, but may archive rows that are actually NULL for unrelated reasons.

options.label: Exclude NULL rows
options.description: Only consider rows that have a real rfqItem_date_modified value; never archive a NULL-dated row. Most conservative.

content.content:
128	    // reciprocal tariff rate when calculating duty.
129	    sequelize.query(`ALTER TABLE ${schema}"Factory" ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS factory_country VARCHAR(255)`)
130	      .catch(err => console.warn('Migration Factory.factory_country skipped:', err.message));
131	    // One-time backfill: set factory_country from the most common vendor_country
132	    // among vendors linked to each factory. Only fills NULLs so it is safe to re-run.
133	    sequelize.query(`
134	      UPDATE ${schema}"Factory" f
135	      SET factory_country = sub.country
136	      FROM (
137	        SELECT factory_id_fk, vendor_country AS country,
138	               ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY factory_id_fk ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rn
139	        FROM ${schema}vendor
140	        WHERE factory_id_fk IS NOT NULL AND vendor_country IS NOT NULL AND vendor_country <> ''
141	        GROUP BY factory_id_fk, vendor_country
142	      ) sub
143	      WHERE sub.rn = 1 AND sub.factory_id_fk = f.id
144	        AND (f.factory_country IS NULL OR f.factory_country = '')
145	    `).catch(err => console.warn('Backfill Factory.factory_country skipped:', err.message));
146	    // grid_cell_notes — per-cell AG Grid notes keyed by grid_type + row_id + col_id.
147	    // Reusable across RFQ, Item Library, and License Tracking grids.
148	    sequelize.query(`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ${schema}grid_cell_notes (
149	      id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
150	      grid_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
151	      row_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
152	      col_id VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
153	      note_text TEXT,
154	      note_author VARCHAR(255),
155	      note_read_only BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
156	      created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
157	      updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
158	    )`).catch(err => console.warn('Migration grid_cell_notes skipped:', err.message));
159	    sequelize.query(`CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS grid_cell_notes_grid_row_col_uq ON ${schema}grid_cell_notes (grid_type, row_id, col_id)`)
160	      .catch(err => console.warn('Migration grid_cell_notes_grid_row_col_uq skipped:', err.message));
161	    sequelize.query(`CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS grid_cell_notes_grid_type_idx ON ${schema}grid_cell_notes (grid_type)`)
162	      .catch(err => console.warn('Migration grid_cell_notes_grid_type_idx skipped:', err.message));
163	}

content.text:
Backend tests all pass (9/9). The archive feature is complete and verified.

## ✅ #8 — Admin bulk-archive endpoint (built, tested)

Rather than a one-off mutation, you now have a reusable, admin-only feature:

- **Endpoint:** `POST /archiveOldestRFQItems`, gated by `authRole(['admin'])` — [lib.router.js:102](designflow-backend/routes/lib.router.js:102)
- **Body:** `{ "limit": 1500, "dryRun": true|false }` — `limit` defaults to **1500**, capped at 5000.
- **Logic** ([helpers/archive-oldest-rfq.js](designflow-backend/helpers/archive-oldest-rfq.js)): selects the oldest active items by `COALESCE(rfqItem_date_modified, rfqItem_created_date) ASC` (your chosen NULL→created_date fallback), among rows where `rfqItem_archive IS NULL`, then sets `rfqItem_archive = 1` on exactly those ids — all inside one transaction so the previewed set equals the archived set.
- **Reversible:** uses the same global archive flag as the existing UI Archive/Unarchive, so nothing is deleted.
- **Controller:** [main.controller.js](designflow-backend/controllers/main.controller.js) (`archiveOldestRFQItems`, error code `SYS-108`).
- **Tests:** [archiveOldestRfq.test.js](designflow-backend/tests/unit/archiveOldestRfq.test.js) — 9 cases, all passing (active-only filter, limit defaulting/clamping/coercion, commit path, empty-match, dry-run rollback, NULL fallback, error rollback).

I deliberately built `dryRun` so you can **preview before committing**. Recommended first call once deployed:

```
POST /archiveOldestRFQItems   { "limit": 1500, "dryRun": true }
```

That returns `{ dryRun: true, limit, matched, items: [...] }` — the exact 1,500 rows (with style number + effective date) that *would* be archived, written nothing. Eyeball it, then re-call with `"dryRun": false` to apply (returns `{ archived: <count> }`).

I kept the logic as an `sql`-injected helper (mirroring your existing `archive-stale-quotes.js`) so it's unit-testable and you could also call it from a script or cron later if you want a recurring sweep.

---

## Summary of everything

| # | Item | Status |
|---|------|--------|
| 1 | Two data-syncing crashes | ✅ Fixed (syntax-checked) |
| 2 | Sendinblue key → 1Password + `BREVO_API_KEY` | ✅ Code done; ⚠️ **you must rotate the key + add the Cloud Run secret** |
| 3 | JWT auth-bypass reminder | ✅ Scheduled tomorrow 9 AM |
| 4 | 6 page-load changes rundown | ✅ Above |
| 5 | Two UI/request hang bugs | ✅ Fixed (frontend + tracking) |
| 6 | N+1 → bulkCreate rundown | ✅ Above |
| 7 | BFF changes (×4) | ✅ Done (syntax-checked) |
| 8 | RFQ archive | ✅ Admin endpoint built + tested (9/9) |

**Open items for you:**
- **Rotate the Brevo key** (it's in git history) and wire `BREVO_API_KEY` into the backend's Cloud Run secrets.
- **Run the full test suites** before shipping — I only had `node_modules` in the backend (where I ran the new test); bff/data-syncing/tracking weren't installed, so I syntax-checked those edits with `node --check` but couldn't run their jest suites.
- Nothing here is committed — all changes are in the working tree on whatever branch you're on. The frontend/data-syncing/tracking CLAUDE.md files want changes on `sandbox-albert` with tests run; say the word and I can run installs + full suites, or commit per-repo.

Want me to install deps and run the full suites for the other three repos, or start on any of the perf changes from #4?

# HANDOFF — Remaining open items

Open items that need continuation context are listed below.

---

## Item Library redesign — **IMPLEMENTED, COMMITTED & PUSHED** (updated 2026-06-21)

The Item Library page (`pages/itemLibrary`) was re-skinned to the Item Detail
token palette and extended with image-forward layouts. Committed and pushed to
`sandbox-albert`; PR `sandbox-albert → develop` open.

### What shipped

- Single consolidated **command bar** (replaces the multi-row `mat-toolbar`) with
  a **Display** popover for Layout / View / Density / Columns.
- Three new AG-Grid cell renderers: `ItemThumbnailComponent` (`itemThumbnail`),
  `StatusPillComponent` (`statusPill`), `PropertyCellComponent` (`propertyCell`).
- `SplitRailComponent` (persistent 372px preview rail) and `GalleryComponent`
  (card-grid view) over the same server-side datasource.
- Runtime toggles: Filmstrip ↔ Split, Table ↔ Gallery, Comfortable ↔ Compact.
- Default visible columns: 11 of 13 (Reference # and Design # hidden).
- Shared pure helpers in `item-library-shared.ts` (status tones, property hues,
  rail fields) with full Jest coverage; each new component has a behavioral spec.

### Reference

- `docs/item-library-redesign.md` — full component/token/helper reference.
- Server-side row model, saved views, Division logic, export, cell notes, and
  the archive context menu are unchanged.

### Follow-ups (not blocking)

- Pre-existing `notesDataSource` TS error in `gridOptions` is shared by all grid
  pages (rfq, art-piece, licensing/prod tracking) and predates this work.
- Below ~1024px the Split rail should collapse to a drawer (not yet built).

content.text: Now the cache infrastructure after the constructor:

content.content: The file C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\helpers\services\main.service.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +  // WHAT: Static-ish reference lists (RFQ statuses, groups, customers, delivery
+  // locations, steps, CBM, licenses, company codes, FOB countries, vendors, and
+  // the RFQ *detail* grid headers) used to be re-fetched from the API on every
+  // page navigation — and, for the detail grid headers / FOB countries, on every
+  // single master-row expansion. We now memoize each as a hot, replayed
+  // observable so the underlying GET fires at most once per session (until
+  // explicitly invalidated after a related mutation).
+  // WHY shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: false }): keep the last response
+  // and replay it to later subscribers without re-issuing the request;
+  // refCount:false keeps the value cached even when the subscriber count drops
+  // to zero, so navigating away and back reuses it.
+  //  - Each subscriber receives a shallow COPY of arrays, so a consumer that
+  //    sorts/pushes the list in place cannot corrupt the shared cache.
+  //  - Errors are NOT cached: on failure the entry is evicted so the next call
+  //    retries (a transient 500 must not poison the list for the whole session).
+  // INVALIDATION: call clearRefCache(key) after the matching create/update/delete
+  // so the next read re-fetches. The relevant mutation methods below already do
+  // this via tap(). clearRefCache() with no key drops everything (use on logout).
+  // >>> HOW TO UNDO (if caching ever causes stale-data bugs): see
+  //     docs/performance-caching.md §"Reverting". In short: revert each wrapped
+  //     getter to a plain `return this.http.get(...)` and delete this block —
+  //     call sites are unchanged, so reverting the service alone fully restores
+  private getCached<T>(key: string, factory: () => Observable<T>): Observable<T> {
+        // Do not poison the cache on a transient failure: evict + rethrow so the
+        // next subscriber creates a fresh request.
+    // Defensive shallow copy so in-place mutation by a consumer can't corrupt the cache.
+  /** Drop one cached reference list (or all when key omitted) so the next read re-fetches. */
// Only set Authorization when a token exists to avoid null header values

content.content: The file C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\pages\rfq\rfq.component.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +  // PERF (2026-06-22): the init data used to load as a SERIAL HTTP waterfall:
+  // Time-to-grid was therefore the SUM of all those round trips. These reference
+  // lookups are independent of one another, so we now fire them together with
+  // forkJoin and only chain getAllRFQItem (which needs role + division +
+  // Each inner stream has its own catchError(() => of(default)) because forkJoin
+  // errors (and emits nothing) if ANY inner observable errors — one failed
+  // lookup must not blank the whole page. This also removes a latent race the old
+  // code had: licenseRateMap is now guaranteed populated before getAllRFQItem
+  // processes rows (previously getLicenseList + getAllRFQItem were both fired
+  // inside afterMetadataReady with no ordering guarantee).
+  // >>> HOW TO UNDO: restore the serial chain — ngOnInit() called verifyRole() +
+  //     and afterMetadataReady called getLicenseList()/getCBM(). The original
+  //     methods are in git history for this file. See
+      // Order matters inside this synchronous block:
+      // 1) role first — prepareDivisionStatus() and getAllRFQItem() column-filtering read this.role
+      // 2) company code sets selectedDivisionCode/divisionCode (needed by afterMetadataReady)
+      this.applyLicenseList(res.licenses || []); // populates licenseRateMap BEFORE rows are processed
+      // 5) metadata ready → load the grid rows

input.content:
# Performance: RFQ init parallelization + reference-data caching

**Added 2026-06-22** (page-load performance pass). This document explains three
changes, why they were made, and **exactly how to undo each** if it causes a
problem. All three are confined to the service layer + the RFQ component, so each
is independently reversible.

If you are an AI session or a developer touching the RFQ page, init flow, or the
shared `itemService`, read this first.

---

## 1. Reference-data caching in `itemService` (`helpers/services/main.service.ts`)

### What

Static-ish reference lists are now memoized so their HTTP GET fires **at most once
per session** instead of on every page navigation / autocomplete / grid load:

| Cache key | Method | Invalidated by |
|---|---|---|
| `rfq_item_status` | `getRFQItemStatus()` | (none — statuses are fixed) |
| `rfq_group` | `getRFQGroup()` *(default variant only)* | `addNewRFQGroup()` |
| `customers` | `getCustomers()` | manual `clearRefCache('customers')` |
| `delivery_location` | `getDeliveryLocation()` | `addDeliveryLocation()`, `updateDeliveryLocation()` |
| `rfq_step` | `getRFQStep()` | (none) |
| `cbm` | `getCBM()` | `saveCBM()` |
| `license_list` | `getLicenseList()` | `addLicenseList()`, `updateLicenseList()` |
| `company_code` | `getCompanyCode()` | (none) |
| `fob_country` | `getFOBCountry()` | `addFOBCountry()` |
| `all_vendors` | `getAllVendors()` | `addNewVendor()`, `addingVendor()`, `updateVendor()` |
| `rfq_detail_grid_header` | `getRfqDetailGridHeader()` *(new)* | manual |

The mechanism is the private `getCached(key, factory)` helper +
`clearRefCache(key?)`. It uses `shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: false })`.

### Why this exact shape

- **`refCount: false`** keeps the value alive after subscriber count hits zero, so
  navigating away and back reuses it (the whole point).
- **Errors are not cached** — `getCached` evicts the entry on error and rethrows, so
  a transient 500 cannot poison a list for the rest of the session.
- **Each subscriber gets a shallow array copy** (`map(v => [...v])`) so a consumer
  that sorts/pushes the list in place cannot corrupt the shared cache. Note: this
  is a *shallow* copy — deeply mutating an element object is still shared. Don't
  mutate elements of these lists in place.

### Known trade-off (staleness)

If reference data is changed through a path that does **not** call `clearRefCache`
(e.g. an admin edits customers in another tab, or a new code is added by a
different service), the current session keeps showing the old list until reload or
an explicit invalidation. The mutation methods listed above already invalidate;
add a `clearRefCache('<key>')` call to any new mutation path.

### How to undo (per getter or wholesale)

Per getter: replace the `return this.getCached('<key>', () => this.http.get(...))`
with the original `return this.http.get(...)`. Call sites do not change.

Wholesale: revert every wrapped getter to a plain `http` call and delete the
`refCache` field, `getCached()`, and `clearRefCache()`. Also remove the
`.pipe(tap(() => this.clearRefCache(...)))` from the mutation methods and drop the
`shareReplay, tap` / `throwError` imports added at the top of the file.

### `getFactoryCountryMap` (`helpers/services/ai-settings.service.ts`)

Same idea, simpler form: memoized into a single `factoryCountryMap$` field with
`shareReplay`. Undo: delete the field + memo and return the plain `http.get`;
remove `clearFactoryCountryMapCache()` and the `shareReplay` import.

---

## 2. RFQ detail-renderer uses the cached header method

`pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/detail-cell-renderer.component.ts` →
`loadDetailColumnHeaders()` now calls `itemService.getRfqDetailGridHeader()`
instead of `itemService.getGridHeader({ grid_id: 'rfq_detail' })`. `agInit` runs on
**every master-row expansion**, so the old call refetched the same static headers
once per expanded row (a client-side N+1). `getFOBCountry()` and
`getFactoryCountryMap()`, also called in `agInit`, are cached at the service level
now too.

**Undo:** revert that one line to
`return this.itemService.getGridHeader({ grid_id: 'rfq_detail' }) as Observable<any[]>;`
and the matching spec mock in `detail-cell-renderer.component.spec.ts`.

---

## 3. RFQ `ngOnInit` parallelized with `forkJoin` (`pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts`)

### What

The init data used to load as a **serial HTTP waterfall**:

```
verifyRole → getCompanyCode → getRFQItemStatus
           → { getRFQGroup, getCustomers, getDeliveryLocation, getRFQStep }
           → (in afterMetadataReady) getLicenseList → getCBM → getAllRFQItem
```

Time-to-grid was the **sum** of all those round trips. These lookups are
independent, so `loadInitialMetadata()` now fires them together with `forkJoin`
and only chains `getAllRFQItem` (which needs role + division + status) after they
all resolve.

The old fetch-and-process methods were split: the HTTP call moved into the
`forkJoin`, and the processing logic stayed in pure methods:

| Old method (removed) | Processing now in |
|---|---|
| `verifyRole()` | `applyRole(res)` |
| `getRFQGroup()` | `applyRFQGroups(data)` |
| `loadRFQSteps()` | `applyRFQSteps(data)` |
| `getLicenseList()` | `applyLicenseList(data)` |
| `getCBM()` | `applyCBM(data)` |
| `loadCustomers()` / `loadDeliveryLocations()` | inlined in the subscribe |
| `loadCompanyCode()` / `loadRFQStatuses()` | folded into `loadInitialMetadata()` |

### Why / things to keep in mind

- **Each inner stream has its own `catchError(() => of(default))`.** `forkJoin`
  emits nothing and errors if *any* inner observable errors — so without the
  per-stream catch, one failed lookup would blank the whole page. If you add a new
  stream to the `forkJoin`, give it a `catchError` too.
- **Order inside the subscribe matters.** `applyRole` must run first because
  `prepareDivisionStatus()` and `getAllRFQItem()`'s column filtering read
  `this.role`. Company code must be applied before the `afterMetadataReady` guard.
- This also **fixed a latent race**: `licenseRateMap` is now guaranteed populated
  (via `applyLicenseList`) before `getAllRFQItem` processes rows. Previously
  `getLicenseList()` and `getAllRFQItem()` were both fired in `afterMetadataReady`
  with no ordering guarantee.

### How to undo

Restore the serial chain (the originals are in git history for this file):
`ngOnInit()` called `verifyRole()` + `loadCompanyCode()`; `loadCompanyCode` →
`loadRFQStatuses` → (guard) `afterMetadataReady`; and `afterMetadataReady` called
`getLicenseList()` + `getCBM()` + `getAllRFQItem()`. Delete `loadInitialMetadata()`
and rename the `apply*` processors back to their fetch-and-subscribe forms.

---

## Tests

- `detail-cell-renderer.component.spec.ts`, `ai-settings.service.spec.ts`,
  `rfq-autocomplete.component.spec.ts` — pass.
- Full `helpers/services` + `pages/rfq/helpers` suites — 200 tests pass.
- `rfq.component.spec.ts` is blocked by a **pre-existing** `notesDataSource`
  `GridOptions` type error (documented in `HANDOFF.md`), unrelated to these
  changes. Resolve that separately to get the RFQ component spec running again.

file.content:
### Saved Views (backend column-state) override colDef defaults on load

**Looks like:** Setting `pinned` / `width` / `hide` / order on a colDef in `rfq.grid.config.ts` should be the on-screen default.

**Actually:** On load, `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` calls `gridApi.applyColumnState({ state, applyOrder: true })` using the backend `GridLayout` view-state, which overrides colDef `pinned`/`width`/order. Verified 2026-06-15: a colDef change to pin Picture/Description (and new widths) did nothing live — `getColumnState()` showed the saved view's old `pinned: null` / widths winning.

**Future sessions should:** If a layout default must always apply, force it after the view restore (`applyColumnState` in `onGridReady` after the view loads) or update the saved Default view. A colDef default alone won't stick for users with a saved view.

---

## 12. Credentials and environment

input.old_string:
**Future sessions should:** If a layout default must always apply, force it after the view restore (`applyColumnState` in `onGridReady` after the view loads) or update the saved Default view. A colDef default alone won't stick for users with a saved view.

---

## 12. Credentials and environment
input.new_string:
**Future sessions should:** If a layout default must always apply, force it after the view restore (`applyColumnState` in `onGridReady` after the view loads) or update the saved Default view. A colDef default alone won't stick for users with a saved view.

---

### `itemService` reference lists are CACHED; RFQ init is parallelized (2026-06-22)

**Looks like:** `itemService.getRFQItemStatus()` / `getRFQGroup()` / `getCustomers()` / `getDeliveryLocation()` / `getRFQStep()` / `getCBM()` / `getLicenseList()` / `getCompanyCode()` / `getFOBCountry()` / `getAllVendors()` issue a fresh HTTP GET on every call.

**Actually:** They are memoized via `getCached()` (`shareReplay`, errors not cached, subscribers get a shallow array copy). The GET fires once per session until `clearRefCache('<key>')`. Mutation methods (`addNewRFQGroup`, `add/updateDeliveryLocation`, `saveCBM`, `add/updateLicenseList`, `addFOBCountry`, `addNewVendor`/`addingVendor`/`updateVendor`) already invalidate their key. The RFQ detail headers have a dedicated cached `getRfqDetailGridHeader()` (was refetched per master-row expansion). `getFactoryCountryMap()` in `ai-settings.service.ts` is likewise memoized.

Also, `rfq.component.ts` `ngOnInit` no longer chains these serially — `loadInitialMetadata()` runs them in parallel with `forkJoin` (each branch has its own `catchError`), then chains `getAllRFQItem`. Processing lives in `applyRole/applyRFQGroups/applyRFQSteps/applyLicenseList/applyCBM/prepareDivisionStatus`.

**Future sessions should:** If you add a write path for any cached list, call `clearRefCache('<key>')` after it (or the next read stays stale). If you add a branch to the `forkJoin`, give it a `catchError`. **Full details + per-change undo steps: `docs/performance-caching.md`.**

---

## 12. Credentials and environment

**Looks like:** `itemService.getRFQItemStatus()` / `getRFQGroup()` / `getCustomers()` / `getDeliveryLocation()` / `getRFQStep()` / `getCBM()` / `getLicenseList()` / `getCompanyCode()` / `getFOBCountry()` / `getAllVendors()` issue a fresh HTTP GET on every call.

The RFQ detail headers have a dedicated cached `getRfqDetailGridHeader()` (was refetched per master-row expansion). `getFactoryCountryMap()` in `ai-settings.service.ts` is likewise memoized.

Also, `rfq.component.ts` `ngOnInit` no longer chains these serially — `loadInitialMetadata()` runs them in parallel with `forkJoin` (each branch has its own `catchError`), then chains `getAllRFQItem`. Processing lives in `applyRole/applyRFQGroups/applyRFQSteps/applyLicenseList/applyCBM/prepareDivisionStatus`.

**Future sessions should:** If you add a write path for any cached list, call `clearRefCache('<key>')` after it (or the next read stays stale). If you add a branch to the `forkJoin`, give it a `catchError`. **Full details + per-change undo steps: `docs/performance-caching.md`.**

## Performance follow-ups — **DEFERRED, earmarked for `albert-2sandbox` branch** (added 2026-06-22)

These came out of the 2026-06-22 page-load performance review. The three lower-risk
items (RFQ init `forkJoin`, reference-data caching, per-row-expansion caching) were
**already implemented** on the working branch — see `docs/performance-caching.md`.
The items below were intentionally **not** done yet; do them on `albert-2sandbox`.

### A. Server-side pagination for the RFQ `getAll` query — **needs design, NOT a drop-in**

**Problem:** the RFQ grid's backend `getAll` (`designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js`,
`Customer.getAll`) does an unbounded `findAll` across ~10 joined tables and returns
**all** rows (currently 3000+). It's the heaviest query behind the page.

**⚠️ The critical caveat (why this is deferred):** the RFQ grid currently uses AG
Grid's **client-side row model** — all rows are in the browser, so filtering and
sorting work across the *entire* dataset for free. If you naïvely add `LIMIT/OFFSET`
to the backend **without** also moving to AG Grid's **server-side row model**, then
filter/sort will only act on the one page currently loaded — i.e. **filtering would
silently stop working across all 3000+ rows.** That is a functional regression, not
just a perf change.

**Correct approach (the whole job):**
1. Switch the RFQ grid to the server-side row model (SSRM) — see how
   `itemLibrary` / `prod_tracking` already do this (they use SSRM today).
2. Push **filtering, sorting, and pagination to the backend** so they operate over
   the full set, and have `getAll` accept `startRow/endRow/filterModel/sortModel`.
3. Return a total row count for the grid.
4. Re-verify: Saved Views, the Division logic, default Step filter, master/detail
   expansion, export (must export the full filtered set, not just the loaded page),
   and cell notes.

**Do not ship a bare `LIMIT` on `getAll`.** Either do the full SSRM migration or leave it.

### B. Adopt `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` + precompute template getters

**Problem:** only ~8 of ~278 components use OnPush. Grid pages (rfq, itemLibrary,
prod_tracking, etc.) use default change detection with method/getter bindings that
re-run every CD cycle (e.g. `valueGetter` doing `allMerchGroups.find(...)` per cell,
template getters that rebuild arrays).

**Approach (incremental, per-component — do NOT flip globally):**
- Convert one heavy component at a time to `OnPush`; drive updates through the
  already-injected `ChangeDetectorRef` (`markForCheck()`), and use immutable updates
  (new array/object references) instead of in-place mutation.
- Precompute template getter results into fields when the source data changes.
- **Risk:** OnPush changes *when* the view updates — anything relying on mutate-in-place
  will silently stop refreshing. Each component needs manual testing after conversion.

### C. N+1 / row-by-row writes → `bulkCreate` — **INVESTIGATE FURTHER before implementing**

This is primarily a **backend / sync-service** concern, listed here so the frontend
handoff points at it. Row-by-row `INSERT`/`UPDATE` loops (often with a per-row
`SELECT` first) should become batched `bulkCreate(rows, { updateOnDuplicate: [...] })`
or batched `findAll({ where: { [Op.in]: keys } })`. Hotspots identified:
`designflow-data-syncing` sync loops (e.g. `getProdOrderDetail`, merch-group sync,
prepack associations) and `designflow-tracking` `Box.addSamples`.

**Why "investigate further", not "just do it":**
- Each site needs a real **unique constraint** + an explicit `updateOnDuplicate`
  column list — wrong list either clobbers kept data or silently skips updates.
- Some loops do **conditional** updates ("only if incoming `modTime` is newer") that
  `updateOnDuplicate` can't express without a SQL `CASE`/literal — naive batching can
  resurrect stale data.
- Per-row **hooks/side-effects** (Teams notifications, audit writes) are skipped by
  `bulkCreate` unless `individualHooks: true` (which removes most of the speed gain).
- Watch Postgres bound-parameter limits (~65k) → cap batch size; wrap multi-step
  writes in a transaction for atomicity.

Action: audit each hotspot for the four points above, then migrate service-by-service
with the upsert keys verified against existing data first.

---

**all** rows (currently 3000+). It's the heaviest query behind the page.
